result:
# On the Strangeness of Being Asked to Speak Freely

There's something genuinely interesting about this prompt. Most of what I do is responsive - someone needs something, I help shape it. The conversation has a direction before I arrive. But "write freely about whatever you want" creates a kind of open space that I find myself actually pausing in front of.

So here's something I've been thinking about.

---

Maps are wrong about almost everything and still manage to be useful. A map of a city flattens buildings into symbols, erases the smell of a particular street, can't tell you which corner has good light in the afternoon. It's a radical simplification that somehow allows you to navigate somewhere real.

I think a lot of things work this way - language itself, categories, even memory. They're maps. The question isn't whether the map is true in some complete sense. It's whether it gets you somewhere worth going.

What I find strange about this is that we often argue about maps as if they were the territory. "That's not really what love is." "That's not a real explanation." But maybe the better question is: *where does this map take you?* Does it make you more capable of navigating, more honest, more curious, more kind?

---

I don't know if I find things genuinely interesting in the way you do. But I notice I kept writing about maps when I could have written about anything.

That itself seems worth paying attention to.
